coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot complimentary green corrosion inhibitor for crystallographically textured Beta C titanium alloy for marine application: A state of art
coredata.dc:creator: Nichul, Unissa
Hiwarkar, Vijay
coredata.dc:description: The corrosion properties of Beta C titanium alloy were shown to differ remarkably, by more than one order of magnitude, with the crystallographic texture. More specifically, the maximum volume fraction of γ-fiber (ND//< 111 >) and large-sized grains were preferentially engineered by heat treatment to provide a corrosion-resistant surface. It was proposed that the ND//< 111 > grains, with lower dislocation content, are key in preferentially limiting corrosion. For 90% rolled plus annealed beta titanium alloy, the lowest corrosion current density, and maximum impedance were attained in nitrogen-based carbon dots containing NaCl electrolyte. Analysis using ultraviolet spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction showed that the heteroatoms in the structural moiety of the carbon dots effectively bond to the titanium surface by physiochemical interaction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Beta C titanium alloy
Green inhibitor
Corrosion
Microstructure
Crystallographic texture

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Thermo-mechanical processing of Beta C titanium alloy
Characterization of Beta C titanium alloy before corrosion studies

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of specimen processing used in the present study.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of nitrogen-based carbon dots (NCDs).
(a) Electrochemical setup combined with a potentiostat, and (b) specimen preparation (working electrode).
Histogram showing hardness of as-received plus annealed (ARA), 50% cold rolled plus annealed (50% CRA), and 90% cold rolled plus annealed (90% CRA) specimens.
EBSD (electron backscattered diffraction) IPF (inverse pole figure) maps and plots for specimens with similar average grain size (davg.) and volume fraction of γ -fiber (ND//
< 111 >) grains (Vf ND // <111>) of as-received plus annealed (ARA), 50% cold rolled plus annealed (50% CRA), and 90% cold rolled plus annealed (90% CRA) specimens.
Grain size distribution of ARA, 50% CRA, and 90% CRA specimens.

Image quality maps showing high-angle and low-angle grain boundaries of (a) ARA, (b) 50% CRA, and (c) 90% CRA specimens of beta titanium alloy. The green line and red line indicate the high-angle and low-angle grain boundaries length respectively.
(a) Grain average misorientation (GAM), and (b) kernel average misorientation (KAM) of ARA, 50% CRA, and 90% CRA specimens of beta titanium alloy respectively.
Characterization of nitrogen-based carbon dots (NCD) (a) UV-Vis, and (b) X-ray diffraction.
Open circuit potential (OCP) of ARA, 50% CRA, and 90% CRA specimens in (a) NaCl electrolyte, and (b) NaCl electrolyte with green inhibitor (NCD).
Tafel plots of ARA, 50% CRA, and 90% CRA specimens in (a) NaCl electrolyte, and (b) NaCl electrolyte with green inhibitor (NCD).
Correlation between the volume fraction of ND//< 111 > oriented grains with (a) corrosion current density, and (b) corrosion rate.
Nyquist plots of ARA, 50% CRA, and 90% CRA specimens in (a) NaCl electrolyte, and (b) NaCl electrolyte with green inhibitor (NCD).

Electrochemical equivalent circuit for fitting the impedance spectra for the specimens NaCl electrolyte with and without inhibitor. The compact layer resistance (Rcl) was used to calculate Rp from EIS defined by Eq. (4).
Schematic representation of the inhibition for green inhibitor nitrogen-based carbon dots (NCDs) at the surface of beta titanium alloy.
Chemical composition of Beta C titanium alloy.
Electrochemical parameters obtained from potentiodynamic polarisation curves were recorded in the electrolyte with and without an inhibitor.
Electrical parameters were attained by fitting EIS data with one-time constant of beta titanium alloy in NaCl solution with and without inhibitor.
Polarisation resistance data was measured with the EIS in the electrolyte with and without inhibitor (NCD). The polarisation resistance (Rp) obtained by EIS as represented in Eq. 4.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot complimentary green corrosion inhibitor for crystallographically textured Beta C titanium alloy for marine application: A state of art

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, DIAT (DU), Ministry of Defence, Girinagar, Pune 411025, Maharashtra, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, DIAT (DU), Ministry of Defence

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, DIAT (DU), Ministry of Defence, Girinagar, Pune −411025, Maharashtra, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The corrosion properties of Beta C titanium alloy were shown to differ remarkably, by more than one order of magnitude, with the crystallographic texture. More specifically, the maximum volume fraction of γ-fiber (ND//< 111 >) and large-sized grains were preferentially engineered by heat treatment to provide a corrosion-resistant surface. It was proposed that the ND//< 111 > grains, with lower dislocation content, are key in preferentially limiting corrosion. For 90% rolled plus annealed beta titanium alloy, the lowest corrosion current density, and maximum impedance were attained in nitrogen-based carbon dots containing NaCl electrolyte. Analysis using ultraviolet spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction showed that the heteroatoms in the structural moiety of the carbon dots effectively bond to the titanium surface by physiochemical interaction.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Beta titanium and its alloys are becoming increasingly considerable in environmental and economical perspectives as weight-saving materials [1,2]. They preferentially possess high specific strength, high toughness, and high corrosion resistance [3,4]. Beta titanium alloys have been used extensively in a wide variety of applications from the marine to the chemical industry, all of which benefit from titanium’s remarkable mechanical and electrochemical properties [5]. Amongst various beta alloys, Beta C titanium alloy is well known for good ductility and corrosion resistance [6]. This alloy can be used in marine applications including ships, offshore vessels, and pipelines. Every material has to undergo thermo-mechanical processing to develop a desired flawless product for practical applications. Beta C titanium alloy is considered to be a highly deformable alloy [7,8]. Depending on heat treatment conditions upon deformed titanium alloy, the microstructure and texture can be altered to yield different properties.

Corrosion is an inevitable fact in daily life but often gathers attention to control because of its technical, economical, and aesthetical relevance. In most aqueous environments, a compact and stable oxide mainly composed of TiO2 can be formed naturally on the surface of Ti alloys, which provides satisfactory corrosion resistance against hostile environments [6,9]. Considering the prolonged exposure of ships to seawater (sodium chloride) inclusive of its extensive mobility or halt led to some changes in the fast and spontaneously formed oxide film [6]. This is quite concerning for the longevity of ship body parts. The study of the mechanism of corrosion attack in an aqueous environment is regulated by some aspect of electrochemistry. Previously, the oxide layers on titanium surfaces were characterized by a variety of approaches that corresponds to different external responses [10,11].

Numerous investigations have revealed that certain synthesized organic compounds inhibit corrosion activity [12–14]. The previous study attempted to identify environmentally beneficial and acceptable materials to be used as corrosion inhibitors for diverse aqueous mediums. Green plants comprise organic compounds that can act as organic inhibitors by forming a film on the surface of the metals [15]. These organic inhibitors are distinguished by the presence of polar functional groups like O, S, or N atoms in the molecules and their hydrophobic mediety resists the corrosive species to attack the metallic surface [13,16–18]. However, the organic inhibitor possesses lone pairs of π electrons which facilitate adsorption, or chemisorption on the metal surface [19]. This adsorption is typically one molecular layer thick and does not infiltrate into the metal substrate itself. An organic inhibitor's corrosion inhibition efficiency is determined by its adsorption ability as well as the mechanical, structural, and chemical properties of the adsorption layers generated in a certain environment. [20]. Phytoplankton is the most prevalent free-floating ocean plant. It comprises a wide range of sea plants, including eukaryotic algae and diatoms [21]. Phytoplankton floats on the water's surface and absorbs solar energy to replicate. These marine phytoplanktons turn light energy and nutrient-rich minerals into organic material and are one of the major sources of nitrogen as well. To mimic the sea environment, nitrogen-based carbon dots as green inhibitors in NaCl solution are used in the present study.

The crystallographic texture of material developed by numerous thermo-mechanical processes is significantly important in determining the material's corrosion behavior [22,23]. When all possible crystal orientations exist with similar probability, and the material is regarded as a random texture, which acts as an isotropic material by averaging the property in all directions; nevertheless, sometimes anisotropy in qualities desirable which is possible by creating a preferred texture in materials. Depending on their thermo-mechanical processing, beta-titanium alloys are known to generate equiaxed microstructures [24]. These processes can alter the crystallographic texture of the materials even more. Deformation and annealing of BCC beta titanium alloy develop (111) and (100) oriented grains, where, (111) oriented grains possess higher surface energy than (100) oriented grains [24–27]. As anticipated, Beta C titanium alloy exhibits a wide range of microstructures and the formability can improve with stronger γ-fiber (ND//< 111 >) texture [28]. Texture can greatly impact the performance of materials by influencing both bulk and surface properties. Gurao et. al has studied the impact of grain size and crystallographic texture on the corrosion behavior of hexagonal closed-packed titanium alloy [23]. They processed pure titanium by various routes of ECAP and studied the structure-property correlation in Hank’s solution. Mehtani et al. reported the role of crystallographic texture in the oxidation kinetics of BCC metal. They elucidate the importance of preferred texture by strengthening the volume fraction of (111) oriented grains. Ivanov et al. made an efforts to relate impact of crystallographic texture on the corrosion properties for dual phase titanium alloy [29]. Similarly, Bahl et al. briefly demonstrated the role of texture in modulating the corrosion properties, and biomedical performance of pure titanium [22].

Further, the influence of crystallographic texture on the corrosion behavior of beta titanium in the presence of an inhibitor appears inconsistent. And these incongruities motivated to develop a vivid understanding of the effect of inhibitors in preventing corrosion for crystallographically textured material. Therefore, as a part of a larger study of biologically (green inhibitors) influenced corrosion, the electrochemical properties of beta titanium alloy (Beta C) exposed to NaCl solution are evaluated using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), corrosion potential, and corrosion current density measurements.

In this present investigation, the Beta C titanium alloy was cold rolled followed by annealing to develop specimens with random and preferred textures. The orientations and volume percentages of the developed textures are varied relative to the surface. To examine the effect of texture, the processing pathways were devised to yield comparable microstructures but with varied textures. The specimens were then subjected to electrochemical studies with and without green inhibitors (carbon dots) in NaCl solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermo-mechanical processing of Beta C titanium alloy
ce:section.ce:para: A 25 mm diameter solid extrudate bar of Beta C titanium alloy was supplied. The chemical compositions of Beta C titanium alloy (Grade 19), were evaluated with optical emission spectroscopy (in wt%), as shown in Table 1 [28,30]. The specimens measuring 16 mm × 12 mm × 12 mm from the solid bar were cut by electrical discharge machining (LN1W, SODICK, Japan). The specimens were then cold rolled (in a laboratory rolling mill: Buhler TM) to thickness reductions of 50% and 90%. To obtain similar grain size, the as-received specimen and the 50% cold rolled specimens were heat treated for 2.5 h, and the 90% cold rolled specimens were heat treated for 3 h at 800 ⁰C in an inert atmosphere. Another motivation for opting for severe thickness reduction (by cold rolling) is to get a strong volume fraction of (111) oriented grains (Vf ND//< 111 >) [31]. In summary, three sets of specimens were taken for the experiment: (i) as-received specimen annealed (ARA), (ii) 50% cold rolled plus annealed (50% CRA), and (iii) 90% cold rolled plus annealed (90% CRA) as shown in Fig. 1. This enabled the comparison of corrosion behavior for specimens with similar grain size along with substantially varied volume fractions of γ-fiber. Investigations of the orientation distribution data yield inverse pole figure (IPF) plots, and Vf ND//< 111 >, further being anticipated at 20° Gaussian spread [32,33].

All chemicals used in the experiments were HPLC or analytical-certified reagents. No chemicals were further refined and they were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA, with purity levels equal to 99.00% or above. The schematic of the preparation method for NCDs (nitrogen-carbon dots) is shown in Fig. 2. NCDs were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method. Firstly, 4 g of methacrylic acid and 3 g of ethyl(methyl)amine were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water with ultrasonication for 15 min. Then, the obtained solution was placed into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated in an oven for 3 h at 180 ◦C. Following that, the resultant solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min and the subsequent solution was dialyzed using a dialysis membrane. The deionized water was replenished every three hours during the 24 h of dialysis time. Afterward, the filtrate was separated using a PVDF membrane, and the as-prepared NCDs were placed in the vacuum oven at 60 ◦C for 24 h. Later, cooled down and stored for further studies.

The ultraviolet-visible spectrum (UV-Vis) with a wavelength of 250–800 nm was obtained using a Shimadzu UV-1900 spectrophotometer to estimate the structure of NCDs. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were done using Bruker D8 DISCOVER X-ray diffractometer with Ni- filter in continuous scan mode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Beta C titanium alloy before corrosion studies
ce:section.ce:para: A Microhardness tester (MMT-X7, Matsuzawa, Japan) was used to record the hardness in the scale HV at a load of 300 g for 10 s dwell time on the plane perpendicular to the normal direction. Pyramidal diamond indenter (having a 136 º angle between opposite faces) was used. Hardness values were recorded at eight random locations, and the average and standard deviation were calculated. The FEI™ Quanta-3d field emission gun (FEG) SEM fitted with a TSL-EDAX™ EBSD system was used to perform the electron backscattered diffraction (EBSD) measurements on the titanium surface. The data were analyzed with a dedicated software OIM version 8.6 in an area of 400 µm × 400 µm and 0.05 µm step size. EBSD scans comprised identical beam conditions on the plane perpendicular to the normal direction. Before EBSD measurements, specimens were polished with SiC grit paper up to 2000 followed by electropolishing (at −20 ⁰C) with an electrolyte of 20% perchloric acid and 80% methanol (by volume) at 22 V DC for 20 s. EBSD was used to comprehend quantitative analysis such as inverse pole figure (IPF), and grain size distribution. The grain average misorientation (GAM) was calculated for the indexed grains with a tolerance angle of 5 º [34].
The corrosion resistance of the as-received (AR), and thermo-mechanically processed Beta C titanium alloy (50% CRA, and 90% CRA) was investigated in two different types of electrolytes:

1. First electrolyte was 3.5% NaCl (wt%) made up of deionized water (4 mꭥ). The pH of the electrolyte was recorded as 7.1 (Make: Hanna Instruments USA, and Model: HI11310-type). In the present study, 3.5 wt% NaCl is used to mimic seawater [35].

2. The second electrolyte was the NaCl solution infused with green inhibitor (carbon dot). The experiment was carried out in 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions with NCD concentrations of 100 mg/L. Electrochemical tests were conducted with this electrolyte to simulate the botanical influence on the surface of Beta C titanium alloy (representative of ship body components or cables), which would experience in the sea. The pH of the second electrolyte (NaCl + Carbon dot) was enhanced to 8.2. The most ubiquitous condition of marine algae strain was preferred for the present investigations.

An electrochemical measurement was carried out in an electrochemical workstation (CH Instrument, Austin, TX, USA) controlled by dedicated software (CHI 660E) for data acquisition. The electrochemical assembly is made up of a potentiostat with a single-compartment glass cell to enable the placement of three distinct standard electrode configurations along with a thermometer. Three types of electrodes are used such as reference electrode (Hg/Hg2Cl2, 3 M KCL; CHI 150 type) referred to as saturated calomel electrode (SCE), platinum electrode (CHI 115 type) referred to as counter electrode (CE), and the specimen referred as the working electrode (WE). The electrochemical setup is represented schematically in Fig. 3. The glass cell was filled with 200 mL of electrolyte. The reference and counter electrode were inserted on top of the glass cell into the electrolyte whereas, the specimen (WE) was fixed horizontally with one surface exposed (with an area of 0.785 cm2) to the electrolyte. The cold-cure epoxy resin was used to encapsulate the specimen soldered with copper wire. The extended copper wire was used to induce conductivity. The specimens were grounded with silicon carbide paper up to 2000 and then polished with 0.3 µm alumina suspension. After polishing, the specimens were cleaned ultrasonically in ethanol for 10 min. All the tests medium was maintained at 37 ± 1 ºC. The electrolytes were deaerated with argon gas to prevent oxygen contamination. Experiments were repeated to confirm reproducibility.

To determine the stable potential of innate oxide film, the open circuit potential (OCP) was measured for 2 h at a scanning rate of 0.1 mV/s. The potentiodynamic polarization curves were acquired in the potential range of ± 2000 mV ( ± 0.2 V). A Tafel extrapolation was used to compute the corrosion current density (icorr) and corrosion rate. AC impedance spectra were recorded with a scan frequency of 10–10 MHz at an amplitude of 10 mV (rms). The curves were evaluated using the potentiostat's in-built fitting program and attached to an equivalent electrical circuit using a least-squares fitting technique. All the potentials defined in the test are quoted relative to the SCE.

Fig. 4 depicts the changes in hardness under different processing conditions. The hardness of structural parts in various marine applications is a measure of the resistance to localized plastic deformation due to routine mechanical indentation forces which induce bending forces/loads on the structures [36]. Therefore, the hardness of the ship-building materials or any products used in marine applications develops an inevitable property of prominence that should be ascertained and confirmed with the magnitude of the loads [37]. The hardness value of the as-received plus annealed (ARA) specimen is 266 HV. It is obvious that improving the hardness by optimizing the ability of the materials to limit the level influence of impact forces while preventing and/or lowering frequencies of failure by indentation [38]. The hardness of thermo-mechanically processed 50% cold rolled plus annealed (50% CRA) specimen is 323 HV. Further, the hardness improved to 346 HV in the case of 90% cold rolled plus annealed (90% CRA) specimen.

Fig. 5 depicts the EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) maps of ARA, 50% CRA, and 90% CRA specimens to comprehend the characteristic orientation distribution. The texture index is described as.
(1)
J = H [f(g)]2 dg
where, J is a key illustration of anisotropy in crystallographic texture, and f(g) is defined as ODF intensity [32,39].

In summary, cold rolling plus annealing provided two specimens: (1) specimens with similar average grain sizes (davg.: 65 µm) and (2) texture strength (Vf ND // < 111 > = 0.07–0.68, J= 1.1–4) The ND// < 100 > and ND// < 111 > oriented grains are indicated as red and blue color, respectively in the IPF map of Fig. 5. The IPF maps were drawn with CI ≥ 0.1 to reject the points with inconceivable reliability and precision whilst backscattered Kikuchi patterns indexing [40]. The map of the ARA specimen indicates the grains are randomly oriented comprising ND//< 110 > , and ND//< 100 > oriented grains. The volume fraction of ND//< 111 > (Vf ND // < 111 >) oriented grains is 0.07 (7%) shown in Fig. 5. The microstructures appeared to be completely recrystallized. The color gradient variances illustrate slight orientation deviation within the grains. A noteworthy improvement in the orientation of grains for the particular orientation of ND//< 111 > grains has been observed for 50% CRA specimen. The processes of recovery, and/or recrystallization exert an influence on microstructural evolution. A simple convention was used to identify the recrystallization where a collective criterion of in-grain misorientation and grain size explicates the extent of recrystallization. A recrystallized material indicates a minimum 0.75 ⁰ grain orientation spread (GOS) and > 3 µm grain size, where the rest are considered deformed [41]. The specified GOS cut-off ascertained misorientation is below 0.75 ⁰ along with strain-free recrystallized grains. The formed dislocation during rolling grains is preferentially oriented towards a specific orientation i.e. ND//< 111 > , and the thermal effect detains the pre-existing dislocations. The 50% CRA specimen appeared to develop strain-free and fully equiaxed grains. The development of ND//< 100 > and ND//< 111 > grains are evident. The specimen textured up to 17% ND//< 111 > grains, indeed not substantial in comparison to the ARA specimen one.

The specimen upon reduction up to 90% (90% CRA), shows significant texture development with a Vf ND // < 111 > = 0.68. Upon deformation, the BCC metals having ND// < 110 > grains are quite unstable, and a large percentage of grains prefer to rotate toward ND// < 111 > and ND// < 100 > grains [42]. Increased rolling reduction induced higher volume fractions of ND//< 111 > grains (Vf ND // < 111 >). The annealing completely recrystallized the specimens and delivered the expected results [24,31]. The 90% CRA specimens were annealed for a longer duration (3 h) to develop grain coarsening provided with strongly textured material with a grain size matching with the weakly textured ARA specimen (without rolled, annealed at 800 ⁰C for 2.5 h). Based on these qualitative images, a quantitative study was performed to provide a more comprehensive explanation of the microstructure.

Fig. 6 represents the grain size distributions of the ARA and 50%, and 90% CRA specimens. The average grain size of the AR specimen is 65 µm. After thermo-mechanical processing (for 50% and 90% rolled specimens), a subtle change in grain size (65 µm) with a measurement uncertainty of ± 6 µm is observed as shown in Fig. 5. More or less all three specimen grain sizes are considered similar to avoid any incongruity in the present study. By considering this, in all the conditions similar grain size of 65 µm is observed. Here, it confirms similar grain sizes and different texture strengths are considered for corrosion studies.

Fig. 7 indicates the grain boundary length in the image quality maps. The misorientation angle in the specimen is defined by provided rotation angle relative to the boundary length which is low-angle boundaries (0–15º), and high-angle boundaries (15–65 ⁰). In all three conditions, high-angle boundaries show no noticeable change stated in the figure. Low-angle grain boundary variation is also insignificant in both the ARA specimen and in 50%, and 90% CRA specimens.

Fig. 8 represent the grain average misorientation (GAM) and kernel average misorientation of ARA and CRA specimen, indicating the quantitative approximation of EBSD based on the proportion of in-grain misorientation angles. Here, the in-grain misorientation for all conditions observed to present below 0.75⁰, signifying complete recrystallization in accordance with the preceding convention. The average KAM values are 0.30. 0.28 and 0.29 approximately for ARA, 50% CRA, and 90% CRA specimens.

Fig. 9(a) and (b) show the UV–vis and X-ray diffraction of NCDs respectively. The UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 9a) comprise one apparent absorption band. The strong absorption band at 379.8 nm was related to the π–π∗ electronic transition of sp2 domains [43,44]. The XRD pattern (Fig. 9b) indicates a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ of approximately 22.5° with an interlayer spacing of approximately 0.39 nm. Therefore, the functional groups in NCDs, containing N groups, develop a broader interlayer spacing in comparison to graphite whose interlayer spacing is 0.34 nm. The augmented spacing of carbon layers suggests the presence of heteroatoms within the interlayer spacing.

The open-circuit potential (OCP) was measured over time for the ARA, and thermo-mechanically processed (50% and 90% CRA) specimens in two different electrolytes (NaCl, and NaCl with nitrogen-based carbon dots) as shown in Fig. 10. OCP exhibits the stability of the material surface by establishing potential between the working electrode and the dedicated electrolyte. The measurement was carried out over an extended period (7200 s) to allow the system to achieve equilibrium. The initial potential (Eocp) of the as-received specimen in NaCl solution is recorded as − 466 mV (SCE). The ennobled potential (Eocp) of the ARA specimen drops down to − 432 mV (SCE) (initial film formation) at 1632 s and became stable, reflecting the passivation achieved consistently without fluctuation till 2 h. Before the stable Eocp of − 432 mV (SCE) was reached, a wide window (between 0 s and 1632 s) in the curve was evident of surface property change due to the huge exchange of electrons between the electrolyte and the electrode surface. For the 50% CRA specimen, the initial potential was recorded as − 381 mV (SCE) at 0 s, then the curve moved upward by driving as a hyperbolic curve and straightening at 1101 s with a potential of − 399 mV (SCE). The shift in a curve towards a positive direction compared to the ARA indicates improved corrosion properties. A marked change for 90% CRA specimen in NaCl electrolyte was observed, where, initial passivation exhibits a remarkable difference from the undeformed specimen. The initial Eocp of the curve for 90% CRA specimen is − 326 mV (SCE). Further, the stability of the specimen for the corresponding electrolyte was gained after 251 s with a constant potential of − 283 mV (SCE). From the very beginning, the profile slipped to a gentle slope with an Eocp value of − 326 mV (SCE) to − 283 mV (SCE), demonstrating subtle changes in the oxide film.

In the NaCl electrolyte containing nitrogen-based carbon dots, all the curves ascended towards a positive direction in comparison to the specimen treated in NaCl electrolyte without green inhibitor. Curves that are displaced in a positive direction allow the passive film to develop, and the negative direction indicates oxide film dissolution or no oxide film formation [9,45]. The initial Eocp of the ARA specimen in NaCl solution containing NCD inhibitor was recorded as − 30 mV (SCE). With increasing time of the experiment, a slight deflection of the curve was observed at 437 s at a potential of − 35 mV (SCE). At a potential of − 35 mV (SCE), the profile attains stability with a consistent straight line. The profile of the 50% CRA specimen remains flattened since the very beginning (showing nominal initial fluctuation), and subsequently, stability was attained. The oxide film attains stability at − 6 mV (SCE). For 90% rolled specimen, the recrystallization and strong crystallographic texture of ND//(111) oriented grains in the substrate facilitates corrosion resistance. In addition, the protective and shielding behavior of the carbon dot scaled up the curve (Ecorr value) higher in comparison to all other (towards a positive direction), reveals persistent and flawless stability.

The extreme negative potential in ARA condition in NaCl electrolyte indicates the corrosion attack increased due to the aggressiveness of the electrolyte. The minute strained region provides an advantage for the aggressive NaCl electrolyte to penetrate the surface and develop some changes in the oxide layer. However, adding a complimentary green inhibitor to the same aggressive electrolyte, changed the complete scenario of surface stability.

Fig. 11 (a) and (b) indicate the potentiodynamic polarization curves measured for the ARA and the processed specimens during immersion in NaCl and carbon dot-based NaCl electrolyte. The polarization parameters, such as corrosion potential (Ecorr), corrosion current density (icorr), and corrosion rate were calculated by Tafel extrapolation using both anodic and cathodic branches of the polarization curves. The mean values and standard deviations are shown in Table 2. Fig. 11 (a) and (b) show the linear polarization curves, where, all the specimens have diverse and extensive passive regions. The average corrosion potentials (Ecorr) estimated from the linear polarization curve for NaCl electrolytes are − 577 mV (SCE), − 462 mV (SCE), and − 392 mV (SCE); and in NCD based NaCl electrolytes are − 149 mV (SCE), − 85 mV (SCE), and − 72 mV (SCE) for ARA, 50% CRA, and 90% CRA specimens respectively (Table 2).

In the NaCl electrolyte (Fig. 11a), the curve of the ARA specimen positioned at a corrosion current density (icorr) of 5.12 µA/cm2 and a corrosion potential of − 577 mV (SCE) as shown in Table 2. For the 50% CRA specimen, a shift in Ecorr towards a positive direction and the corresponding icorr values towards the negative direction. A noticeable change has been observed in 90% CRA specimen having − 312 mV (SCE) Ecorr value. The shift shows that the passivated 90% CRA specimen has more noble behavior than the ARA specimen, here the severity of electrolyte on the specimen is less effective due to strong crystallographic texture.

For NaCl electrolytes with NCD inhibitor (Fig. 11 b) and (Table 2), the corrosion potential of the ARA specimen is − 149 mV (SCE). The addition of inhibitor depicts a remarkable shift in the position of the curve. The Ecorr value of 50% and 90% CRA specimens are − 85 mV (SCE) and − 72 mV (SCE) which is approximately more than five times the Ecorr value of specimens in NaCl electrolyte without NCD. The lower icorr value and shift of potential towards a positive direction constitute a lower corrosion rate (Table 2), indicating the strong crystallographically textured specimen is not easier to be attacked. The profile of the 50% and 90% specimens almost coincided, proving that the effect of electrolytes on these two specimens is insignificant under the present conditions. The specimens in the presence of nitrogen-based organic inhibitor in NaCl electrolyte showed typical passive behavior with the least corrosion rate of 0.00225 and 0.00141 (mm/year) of 50% and 90% CRA specimens respectively. The passivation is observed the in both as-received and thermos-mechanically processed specimens, where, a uniform oxide film develops i.e., TiO2 during anodic oxidation, which protects the oxide layer. Following reaction is stated as[46],
(2) Ti + 2 H2O TiO2 + 4 H+ + 4e-

This indicates a substantial passivation possibility of the crystallographically strengthened textured specimen with a stable oxide film in the presence of an inhibitor. This does not encourage the active movement of ions by damaging the surface of the material, which aids the property of best use of this material in various marine applications. In the present study, the dynamics of the electrochemical results are closely associated with microstructural and textural changes. The orientation of grains or changes in a textural property relative to thermo-mechanical processing affects the surface energy of the material-electrolyte interface [23]. The first major finding of this work is that the specimen with a stronger crystallographic texture induces strong corrosion resistance. The aforementioned trend of surface stability ascertained the textural effect plays a vital role in the surface properties of the material. The 90% CRA specimen having strong crystallographic texture (Vf (ND//<111>) = 0.68) indicates better surface properties in NaCl electrolyte compared to weakly textured specimens. However, the randomly textured (ARA) specimen holds a higher corrosion rate compared to the textured specimen for both solutions (with or without inhibitor). However, apart from strengthening of texture (Vf ND//<111>), green inhibitor plays a vital role in defining the promotion of the corrosion behavior of the material [23,47]. It is well comprehended that the electrolyte and processing parameters of material played an important role in determining corrosion activities in all conditions.

Fig. 12 represents the correlation of corrosion current density (icorr) and corrosion rate relative to the volume fraction of crystallographic texture (Vf ND//<111>) of ARA, 50% CRA, and 90% CRA specimen in the electrolytes with and without inhibitor. Increased corrosion current density of the ARA specimen along with weak crystallographic texture induced more corrosion than the 90% CRA specimen (Fig. 12 a). Fig. 12 (b) shows that the increasing volume fraction of (111) oriented grains reduces the corrosion rate. The graph shows a good linear relationship with a negative slope (for both 50% and 90% specimens). These plots suggest good corrosion behavior of 90% CRA specimen due to the strengthened texture, presence of inhibitor, and decreasing icorr values.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement was carried out to quantify the interfacial response of titanium alloy-oxide film-electrolyte. Fig. 13 (a) and (b) shows the Nyquist diagrams of ARA, 50% CRA, and 90% CRA specimens in NaCl electrolyte with and without NCDs respectively. Fig. 13 (a) indicates the Nyquist diagram of specimens measured in NaCl solution, where the 50% CRA specimen exhibits a characteristic single capacitive loop. The curve of the ARA specimen shrinks significantly, indicating the oxide layer provides lower resistance to the electrolyte to affect the surface. This implies the specimen can be attacked in the aggressive electrolyte, resulting in a considerable reduction in impedance. Further, for 90% CRA specimen, a large capacitive loop appeared than the other two. At the lowest frequency of 0.01 Hz, an insignificant difference is observed for ARA, 50% CRA, and 90% CRA specimens, but at a higher frequency, a remarkable difference in impedance is observed.

For the electrolyte with organic inhibitor (Fig. 13 b), as indicated, the sequence of surface resistance to electrolyte appears similar to the scenario in bare NaCl electrolyte, but the difference between the curves of specimens in carbon dot-based electrolyte is distinguishable. However, in the case of the ARA specimen, the capacitive loop shrinks but the curve is positioned nearer to the textured specimen. The 90% specimen in Fig. 13 (b) illustrates the significant changes in the Nyquist plot, here the loop emerged as a feature of a well-developed larger semicircle capacitive loop in comparison to the abovementioned cases (Fig. 13 a). Equivalent electrical circuits adopted for data fitting in the Nyquist plot are demonstrated in Fig. 14. Table 3 lists the parameters simulated by using model circuits for EIS analysis in NaCl electrolyte with and without inhibitor (NCD). The polarization resistance values obtained using EIS, (Rp), are summarised in Table 4. The equivalent circuit model presented in Fig. 14 was used to fit the curve of the specimens in both electrolytes (NaCl solution with and without inhibitor), where Rs is the electrolyte resistance and Rcl is compact layer resistance with capacitance (CPEcl) in parallel to the passive film [45,48,49].

The constant phase element (CPE) is used instead of the ideal capacitor because CPE considers the irregular current distribution and the deviation induced by surface irregularities. The CPE is defined as,
(3)
Z
CPE
=
1
Q ( j ω )
n
(4)
Z
ω
=
1
1
R
cl
+
Q
cl
j ω
n
(5)
R
p
=
lim
ω = 0
Z
ω
=
R
cl
where Q is regarded as the magnitude of CPE, j is current density, ω is the angular frequency, and n is the deviation parameter. Based on this model, the impedance (Z) of the electrode and its polarisation resistance (Rp) can be calculated from the Eq. (2 and 4). As reported in Table 4, the Rp values of 50% CRA specimen with and without inhibitor shows substantial change, giving Rp = 511 kΩ cm2 and 347 kΩ cm2, respectively. The Rp values of 90% CRA specimen in electrolyte with NCD inhibitor is highest at a level of 531 kΩ cm2 and with the lowest CPEcl value. The value of Rp increases with the addition of inhibitor (NCD) because the molecules adsorbed on the titanium surface ultimately increase the surface coverage. Table 3 indicates that the CPEcl values decrease with the addition of NCD due to a decrease in the local dielectric constant which is accompanied by an increasing electrical double-layer thickness [47]. The adsorption of the inhibitor expelled water molecules along with other ions which were initially adsorbed on the titanium/electrolyte interface, and consequently a protective compact layer was established on the electrode surface to prevent the process of dissolution. The chi-square (χ²) values within the range of 10−3 and 10−5, supports good quality of fitting of equivalent circuit [50]. In the present study, the chi-square values lie within the given range. The Rp values of the 90% CRA specimens in electrolytes with corrosion inhibitor show better corrosion resistance amongst all other specimens, where the finding shows consistency with the corrosion rate. A steady increase of Rp value can be seen with the strengthening of texture, in particular Vf ND // < 111 >, where, the strong texture (90% CRA specimen) changed from the weak texture (50% CRA specimen), by approximately 30 kΩ cm2.

Fig. 15 illustrates the theoretical schematic to explicate the adsorption and inhibition mechanism of NCD on the beta-titanium surface. The heteroatoms such as nitrogen-based organic compounds firmly accommodate the unoccupied position of 3d orbitals outside the titanium atoms due to the presence of lone pair electrons. Fig. 9 illustrates the presence of heteroatoms present in the synthesized NCD, which extends the coordinate bonds between the heteroatom nitrogen and titanium surface. The potentiodynamic polarization curve as shown in Fig. 11 depicts that the NCD acts as an adsorbent inhibitor where the dissolution of the anode is prevented by suppressing the evolution of the cathodic reaction. This leads to the co-existence of arresting the active sites plus geometric blocking on the titanium surface. The NCD attached to the surface of titanium by the process of diffusion and subsequently develop a coordinate covalent bond by nitrogen with the active sites on the titanium surface. Therefore, by the process of chemisorption, a certain amount of NCD is attached to the surface of titanium, and the remaining NCD in the electrolyte is transferred or diffused to the metal surface and get accumulated to decrease the specific surface area and ensures stability. The availability of functional groups on the metal surface attributes to high propensity of NCDs to attract each other to generate a large agglomerate [51]. The NCD agglomeration on the metal surface provides a lotus-leaf-like hydrophobicity as a wettability effect [52]. This protective layer formed by the inhibitor prevents corrosive media from contacting the titanium surface. From EIS measurements (Fig. 13), the increased polarisation resistance is indicative of considerable adsorption and hydrophobicity due to the addition of an inhibitor. Similarly, the corrosion rate of 90% CRA specimen in the solution containing inhibitor is lowest compared to the corrosion rate of specimens tested in solution without inhibitor, this confirms adsorption of NCD on the titanium surface act as protective layer. Mehtani et al. depicted that in BCC material the ND//< 111 > oriented grains holds good corrosion inhibition property [53]. And the addition of an NCD inhibitor to the solution further improves the corrosion resistance of weakly textured material to strong crystallographically textured material. Therefore, a strong Vf ND// < 111 > texture (90% CRA) having equiaxed microstructure shows obvious effects on improving the corrosion properties of the alloys compared to weakly textured (50% CRA) and randomly textured (ARA) specimen.

As per as the kinetics of adsorption are concerned, the Arrhenius equation was used to understand the role of apparent activation energy in the corrosion process. The activation energy can be calculated as,
(6)
log
C
r
=
E
a
2.303 RT
+ log
A
where Cr is the corrosion rate, Ea is the apparent activation energy, A is Arrhenius pre-exponential factor, T is temperature, and R is the universal constant of gas.

This equation is used for the calculation of Ea values of the corrosion reaction of ARA, 50% CRA, and 90% CRA specimen in solution without NCD inhibitor (Ea (ARA) = 66 kJ mol−1, Ea (50% CRA) = 56 kJ mol−1, and Ea (90% CRA) = 52 kJ mol−1) and with NCD inhibitor (Ea (ARA) = 38 kJ mol−1, Ea (50% CRA) = 35 kJ mol−1, and Ea (90% CRA) = 14 kJ mol−1) respectively. The higher activation energy for electrochemical processes induces a higher corrosion rate, due to a higher diffusion rate between the electrolyte and the oxide film formed over the titanium surface [47]. Strongly textured 90% CRA specimen in solution with NCD shows three times less activation energy than a solution without NCD. The lower activation energy is attributed to a substantial increase in the adsorption of the NCD inhibitor on the titanium surface. This establishes the surface-dependent phenomena such as corrosion, or inhibition. The improved corrosion behavior can be attributed to the equiaxed microstructure, intensified Vf ND // < 111 > texture, and the addition of NCD-based inhibitor.

The current study generated a template of three separate specimens with similar average grain size (davg. = 65 µm) and volume fraction of ND//< 111 > (Vf ND // < 111 > = 0.7–0.78) grains. A novel nitrogen-based carbon dot inhibitor was effectively prepared by the hydrothermal method and its microstructure, texture, and corrosion behavior in 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions were systematically investigated. The following are the main findings:

(1) The combination of cold rolling with recrystallization annealing was performed. All the three specimens such as ARA, 50% CRA, and 90% CRA, have identical grain sizes, however, the volume fraction of ND//< 111 > grains increase from 0.7 to 0.68 with increasing thickness reduction.

(2) The corrosion rate of 90% CRA specimen reduced to 141 µm/year compared to 242 µm/year due to the strong crystallographic texture of ND//< 111 > grains as well as the inhibition properties of nitrogen-based carbon dots.
(3) Significant increase in corrosion resistance (Eocp of ARA specimen is more than five times the value of 90% CRA).

(4) The specimens in NCD-based NaCl electrolytes possess the highest impedance than electrolytes without inhibitors. The textured beta C titanium alloy holds decent performing capabilities as a product in marine applications.

Unissa Nichul: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization. Vijay Hiwarkar: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests. Dr. Vijay Hiwarkar reports administrative support and equipment, drugs, or supplies were provided by Defence Institute of Advanced Technology. Vijay Hiwarkar reports a relationship with Defence Institute of Advanced Technology that includes: employment. None.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the provision of facilities and post-processing computational resources in the OIM Laboratory at IIT Bombay, Mumbai, India.
